walkthrough.title: Place the Dice Board
walkthrough.text: Welcome, Abbot. Let us prepare the scriptorium. Begin by placing the small game board in the center of the table. This board holds the five category dice, one for each type of sacred work in your monastery's library: pigments, holy books, manuscripts, forbidden tomes, and monks.

walkthrough.title: Set the Category Dice
walkthrough.text: Take the five colored dice and place each one on its matching space on the board. Set every die to show the value 3. These dice represent how many victory points each category is worth at the end of the game. Their values will shift during play as abbots use Church cards to manipulate them, so no category's worth is fixed.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Deck
walkthrough.text: Gather all 87 cards into a single deck and shuffle them thoroughly. The deck contains three types of cards: colored category cards with values from 1 to 4, gold cards with values from 1 to 3, and Church cards that let you adjust dice values. Everything goes into one shared deck.

walkthrough.title: Remove Cards by Player Count
walkthrough.text: Based on the number of abbots at the table, you must remove some cards from the game unseen. For 2 players, remove 22 cards face down. For 3 players, remove 15 cards. For 4 players, remove 8 cards. Set these aside without looking at them. They remain secret for the entire game, creating uncertainty about which cards are still available.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Starting Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player. The youngest abbot may go first, or you may select randomly. No cards are dealt to anyone at the start. Your entire hand will be built during the Gift Phase through careful drafting. Place the deck within reach of the starting player, and you are ready to begin.

bullets: Place the small game board in the center of the table
Board holds the 5 category dice

bullets: Place 5 colored dice on matching board spaces
Set each die to 3 (values can change during the game via Church cards)

summary.title: Shuffle the Deck
bullets: Shuffle all 87 cards into one deck
Card types: category cards (values 1-4), gold cards (values 1-3), Church cards

summary.title: Remove Cards by Player Count
bullets: 2 players: remove 22 cards face down
3 players: remove 15 cards face down
4 players: remove 8 cards face down
Removed cards stay secret — never revealed

summary.title: Determine the Starting Player
bullets: Youngest player or random selection goes first
No cards are dealt — hands built during the Gift Phase

walkthrough.title: Two Phases of Play
walkthrough.text: Biblios unfolds across two distinct phases. First comes the Gift Phase, where abbots draft cards from the shared deck to build their hands and fill the auction pile. Then comes the Auction Phase, where the contents of the auction pile are bid upon one at a time. Understanding both phases is essential before your first game.

walkthrough.title: Gift Phase: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: During the Gift Phase, the active abbot draws cards one at a time from the top of the deck. The number of cards drawn equals the number of players plus one. In a 2-player game, draw 3 cards. In a 3-player game, draw 4. In a 4-player game, draw 5. Each card must be assigned immediately before drawing the next one.

walkthrough.title: Gift Phase: Assigning Each Card
walkthrough.text: For each card you draw, you must immediately choose one of three destinations. You may keep it by placing it face down in your hand, but you may only keep exactly one card per turn. You may give it face up to an opponent, and each opponent must receive exactly one card per turn. Or you may place it face down on the auction pile, and exactly one card must go there per turn. The tension is fierce: you assign each card blind to what comes next.

walkthrough.title: Gift Phase: Playing Church Cards
walkthrough.text: Church cards are special. If you have a Church card in your hand at any point during your turn in either phase, you may play it as a free action. When you play a Church card, choose any one category die on the board and adjust it up or down by exactly 1, within the range of 1 to 6. This changes how many victory points that category is worth. You may play Church cards immediately upon keeping them during the Gift Phase.

walkthrough.title: Gift Phase Ends
walkthrough.text: After the active player assigns all their drawn cards, the next player clockwise becomes the active player and draws their own set of cards. This continues around the table until the entire draw deck is exhausted. Once the last card from the deck has been assigned, the Gift Phase is over and the Auction Phase begins.

walkthrough.title: Auction Phase: Revealing Cards
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the auction pile thoroughly. Then reveal cards from the top one at a time. Each revealed card is auctioned off before the next one is flipped. The player to the left of the current auctioneer bids first, and bidding proceeds clockwise. The role of auctioneer rotates clockwise after each auction.

walkthrough.title: Auction Phase: Bidding on Category and Church Cards
walkthrough.text: When a category card or Church card is revealed, players bid using gold cards from their hand. Bids must be at least 1 gold in value. Going clockwise, each player may raise the bid or pass. Once you pass, you are out of that auction. The highest bidder wins the card and discards the gold cards they bid. If nobody bids, the auctioneer takes the card for free.

walkthrough.title: Auction Phase: Bidding on Gold Cards
walkthrough.text: Here is the twist that gives Biblios its bite. When a gold card is revealed from the auction pile, players bid using their category cards instead. The highest total value of discarded category cards wins the gold. This reversal is critical. You are spending your scoring cards to gain auction currency. Every gold card won costs you points elsewhere, so bid wisely.

walkthrough.title: Scoring: Who Wins Each Die
walkthrough.text: After every card in the auction pile has been resolved, scoring begins. For each of the five categories, every player totals the values printed on their cards of that color. The player with the highest total in a category claims that category's die. In case of a tie, no one wins that die. It remains unclaimed.

walkthrough.title: Scoring: Determining the Winner
walkthrough.text: Each claimed die is worth the number of victory points currently showing on its face, which may have been changed by Church cards throughout the game. Add up the values of all dice you won. The abbot with the highest total is the victor. If two players are tied in total die value, the player who won more individual dice wins. If still tied, the abbots share in their glory.

summary.title: Two Phases of Play
bullets: Phase 1: Gift Phase (card drafting from deck)
Phase 2: Auction Phase (bid on the auction pile)

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Scriptorium
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we shall walk through a 3-player game between Abbot Aldric, Abbot Beatrice, and Abbot Cassius. Three players is the recommended count for Biblios — it strikes the best balance between competition and card flow. We will cover both phases and highlight common mistakes new abbots make.

walkthrough.title: Setting the Stage
walkthrough.text: The dice board is placed with all five dice set to 3. Fifteen cards have been removed unseen from the 87-card deck. No one knows which categories were thinned. Abbot Aldric is the starting player. In a 3-player game, the active player draws 4 cards per turn: keep 1, give 1 to each of the other 2 players, and place 1 on the auction pile.

walkthrough.title: Aldric's First Draw
walkthrough.text: Aldric draws his first card: a blue Holy Books card worth 3. That is a strong card. Should he keep it now, or gamble that something better is coming? Aldric decides to keep it immediately. He draws the second card: a gold card worth 2. Not bad, but he already used his keep. He places the gold face up in front of Beatrice. Third card: a red Pigments card worth 4. Aldric groans and must give it to Cassius. Fourth card: a green Manuscripts card worth 1. This goes to the auction pile.

walkthrough.title: The Agony of Early Commitment
walkthrough.text: Notice what happened. Aldric kept a decent card early, then watched a gold card and a high-value pigments card slip through his fingers. This is the core tension of the Gift Phase. Every card must be assigned the moment you see it. You cannot peek ahead. Sometimes you keep early and regret it. Sometimes you give away early and regret that instead. Embrace the uncertainty — it is what makes the Gift Phase so engaging.

walkthrough.title: Beatrice Drafts Wisely
walkthrough.text: Beatrice draws her four cards. First: a gold card worth 1. She sends it to the auction pile since 1-gold is weak. Second: a purple Forbidden Tomes card worth 3. She keeps it. Third: a Church card. She gives it to Aldric, reasoning that Aldric might use it to raise a category she does not care about. Fourth: a yellow Monks card worth 2. She gives it to Cassius. Beatrice kept a strong category card and avoided giving away anything too valuable.

walkthrough.title: Cassius and the Gift Phase Continues
walkthrough.text: Cassius takes his turn, and the Gift Phase continues around the table. Over many rounds, hands fill up. Aldric is collecting blue Holy Books and some gold. Beatrice is focusing on purple Forbidden Tomes and red Pigments. Cassius has a pile of yellow Monks cards and some gold. The auction pile grows steadily with each turn.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Church Cards
walkthrough.text: Aldric received a Church card from Beatrice earlier but has been holding it, unsure when to use it. Several turns pass. Meanwhile, Cassius plays a Church card to raise the yellow Monks die from 3 to 4. This is a common beginner mistake: hoarding Church cards without purpose. Church cards are free actions. If you are collecting a category, raise its die immediately to increase its value. If an opponent is winning a category, lower it to reduce their payoff.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Church Cards Proactively
walkthrough.text: Church cards are among the most powerful tools in Biblios. Raising a die you are winning from 3 to 4 is like adding a free victory point. Lowering a die your opponent dominates from 3 to 2 costs them a point. Do not sit on Church cards hoping for the perfect moment. The perfect moment is usually now. Use them to shape the scoring landscape in your favor throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: The Gift Phase Ends
walkthrough.text: After many rounds of drafting, the draw deck runs out. The Gift Phase is over. Each player has a hand of category cards, gold cards, and perhaps a Church card or two. The auction pile is a mysterious stack of face-down cards. Everyone contributed to it, and everyone remembers some of what they put there — but not everything. Now the real bidding war begins.

walkthrough.title: Auction Phase Begins
walkthrough.text: The auction pile is shuffled and the first card is revealed: a red Pigments card worth 4. Beatrice wants it badly — she has been collecting Pigments. Aldric opens with a bid of 1 gold. Beatrice raises to 2 gold. Cassius passes. Aldric thinks about it and raises to 3 gold. Beatrice grits her teeth and bids 4 gold. Aldric passes. Beatrice wins the card but spent two gold cards totaling 4 value.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Overbidding in Auctions
walkthrough.text: Beatrice spent 4 gold on a single card worth 4 points in Pigments. But is it worth it? If the Pigments die is only at 3, and she was already winning Pigments without this card, she overpaid. She burned gold that she could have used on two cheaper cards later. New players often overbid on cards they want emotionally rather than calculating whether the cost is justified by the die's current value.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Bid Based on Die Value and Need
walkthrough.text: Before bidding, check two things. First, what is the die currently worth for that category? A category die at 5 or 6 is worth fighting for. A die at 1 or 2 might not be worth a single gold. Second, do you actually need this card to win the category, or are you already ahead? Spend gold only where it changes the outcome. Every gold card spent is one fewer bid in future auctions.

walkthrough.title: A Gold Card Appears at Auction
walkthrough.text: The next auction card is a gold card worth 3. Remember, gold cards at auction are paid for with category cards, not gold. Cassius bids a yellow Monks card worth 1. Aldric bids a green Manuscripts card worth 2. Cassius raises with another Monks card, bringing his total to 3. Aldric passes. Cassius wins the gold card but sacrificed two Monks cards totaling 3 points from his collection.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Overspending Category Cards for Gold
walkthrough.text: Cassius spent 3 points worth of Monks cards to gain a 3-value gold card. But those Monks cards were contributing to his majority in yellow Monks. If another player now ties or beats him in that category, Cassius loses the Monks die entirely. Bidding category cards for gold is always a painful trade. Only do it if you have a surplus in that category or if you desperately need gold for a critical upcoming auction.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Guard Your Majorities
walkthrough.text: In Biblios, you win dice by having the highest card total in each color. Every category card you spend on gold auctions weakens your position. Before bidding category cards, mentally check: can I still win this category if I give up these cards? If the answer is uncertain, hold your cards. It is better to skip a gold card than to lose a die worth 3 to 6 victory points.

walkthrough.title: The Final Auctions and Church Cards
walkthrough.text: As the auction pile dwindles, a Church card is revealed. Aldric wins it with a bid of 2 gold and immediately plays it, raising the blue Holy Books die from 3 to 4. He has been collecting Holy Books all game. This last-minute adjustment means the die he is about to win is now worth 4 instead of 3. Church cards auctioned late in the game can be decisive. Never ignore them.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Game
walkthrough.text: The auction pile is empty. Time to score. Each abbot totals their card values in each of the five colors. Aldric wins Holy Books with 8 total versus Beatrice's 5 and Cassius's 3 — he claims the blue die showing 4. Beatrice wins Pigments at 10 and Forbidden Tomes at 7, claiming dice worth 3 and 3. Cassius wins Monks at 6, claiming the die at 4. No one wins Manuscripts outright due to a tie. Aldric: 4 points. Beatrice: 6 points. Cassius: 4 points. Beatrice, Abbot of the Finest Library, wins.

walkthrough.title: Reflecting on the Game
walkthrough.text: Beatrice won by committing to two categories early and using Church cards to protect her interests. Aldric focused too narrowly on one category and held his Church card too long. Cassius weakened his Monks majority by spending cards on gold he did not need. The lessons are clear: commit to two or three categories, use Church cards aggressively, and guard your majorities in the Auction Phase.

walkthrough.title: Key Takeaways
walkthrough.text: Here is what to remember from this practice session. First, the Gift Phase is about controlled uncertainty — assign each card wisely knowing you cannot see ahead. Second, Church cards are free and powerful — use them to raise your dice and lower your opponents' dice. Third, in the Auction Phase, bid only when the die value and your need justify the cost. Fourth, never sacrifice category cards for gold unless your majority is safe.

walkthrough.title: Before You Enter the Monastery
walkthrough.text:
Before you begin your own game of Biblios, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for guidance on which categories to pursue and how to value Church cards.

Second, browse the Appendix for a quick reference on card types, bidding rules, and scoring details.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. May your library be the envy of all Christendom!

summary.title: Before You Enter the Monastery
bullets: Check out General Tips for category and Church card strategy
Browse the Appendix for card types, bidding rules, and scoring
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Focus on Two or Three Categories
walkthrough.text: The most common mistake new abbots make is spreading their collection across all five categories. With only so many cards available, you cannot win every die. Instead, commit to two or three colors early in the Gift Phase and funnel your efforts there. Winning two dice worth 4 and 5 is far better than losing all five categories by one card each. Let go of categories that are not working and double down on those that are.

walkthrough.title: Church Cards Are Your Secret Weapon
walkthrough.text: Church cards can shift a die up or down by 1. This may seem small, but it is enormous in a game decided by single victory points. Raising a die you are winning from 3 to 5 over two Church cards nets you 2 extra VP for free. Lowering a die your opponent dominates from 3 to 1 saves you from conceding 2 VP. Play Church cards early and with purpose. Raise your strong categories and crush your opponents' best categories.

walkthrough.title: Remember What You Put in the Auction Pile
walkthrough.text: During the Gift Phase, you place one card face down on the auction pile every turn. Pay attention to what goes there. If you sent three gold cards to the pile, you know the Auction Phase will have gold available. If you buried high-value category cards there, you know competition will be fierce. Your memory of the auction pile gives you an information edge when bidding.

walkthrough.title: Gold Is Only Useful for Bidding
walkthrough.text: Gold cards have zero value at the end of the game. They exist solely as auction currency. Do not hoard gold past the Auction Phase — spend every last coin. Conversely, do not undervalue gold during the Gift Phase. A hand with no gold means you cannot win a single auction for category cards or Church cards. Aim to enter the Auction Phase with a healthy reserve of gold, then spend all of it.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Opponents Collect
walkthrough.text: During the Gift Phase, you give cards face up to opponents, so everyone sees some of what others are collecting. Pay close attention. If Beatrice is stacking red Pigments, you can lower the Pigments die with a Church card or stop bidding on Pigments in the Auction Phase. If no one seems to be collecting green Manuscripts, you might sneak a majority there with just a few cards. Information is power in the monastery.

walkthrough.title: Gift Phase Assignment Strategy
walkthrough.text: When deciding how to assign each drawn card, follow a simple priority. If the card is great for you and you have not kept one yet, keep it. If the card is terrible for everyone, send it to the auction pile. If you must give a card to an opponent, give the one that helps them least. Avoid giving strong cards in a color an opponent is already collecting. And always send low-value gold and unwanted category cards to the auction pile.

walkthrough.title: Auction Phase Discipline
walkthrough.text: In the Auction Phase, set a mental budget before bidding starts. Decide which categories you need to reinforce and how much gold you are willing to spend on each. Let cards you do not need go cheaply to opponents. If a bidding war erupts over a card that does not help your main categories, let the other abbots bleed each other dry. Your gold is better spent on the cards that actually swing your majorities.

walkthrough.title: Ties Are Your Enemy
walkthrough.text: Remember that tied categories mean no one wins the die. If you suspect you and an opponent are neck and neck in a color, you must either pull ahead or abandon that category entirely. Investing cards and gold into a tie is the worst outcome — you spent resources and got zero VP. When in doubt, one extra card in a contested category is worth more than two cards in a category you are already winning comfortably.

summary.title: Focus on Two or Three Categories
bullets: Do not spread across all 5 colors — commit to 2-3
Winning two dice at 4-5 VP beats losing all five by 1 card
Abandon categories that are not working

summary.title: Church Cards Are Your Secret Weapon
bullets: Raise dice you are winning, lower dice opponents dominate
Two Church cards on one die = 2 extra VP (or 2 fewer for opponent)
Play them early with clear purpose

summary.title: Remember What You Put in the Auction Pile
bullets: Track what you sent to the auction pile during the Gift Phase
Memory gives you an edge when bidding

summary.title: Gold Is Only Useful for Bidding
bullets: Gold = 0 VP at game end — spend it all during auctions
Enter the Auction Phase with enough gold to compete
Do not hoard gold, do not ignore gold

summary.title: Watch What Opponents Collect
bullets: Cards given face up reveal opponents' strategies
Lower dice for colors opponents dominate
Exploit uncontested categories with minimal investment

summary.title: Gift Phase Assignment Strategy
bullets: Keep great cards early if you have not used your keep
Send low-value or unwanted cards to the auction pile
Give opponents cards that help them least

summary.title: Auction Phase Discipline
bullets: Set a mental gold budget before auctions begin
Only bid on cards that reinforce your target categories
Let opponents bleed each other on cards you do not need

summary.title: Ties Are Your Enemy
bullets: Tied categories = no one wins the die = wasted resources
Invest one extra card in contested categories to break ties
Abandon a tie if cost exceeds the die's value

walkthrough.title: The Die Manipulation Engine
walkthrough.text: The most powerful advanced strategy in Biblios revolves around Church cards. Instead of collecting the highest card totals, focus on winning two or three cheap categories while inflating their dice to 5 or 6. If you raise two dice to 5 each and win them with modest card totals, you score 10 VP while opponents who won more expensive categories at lower die values fall short. Pair this with lowering dice in categories you cannot win.

walkthrough.title: Auction Pile Seeding
walkthrough.text: During the Gift Phase, you control what goes into the auction pile. Advanced players seed the pile strategically. Send cards there that you want to buy cheaply later — cards in categories your opponents are not collecting. Your opponents will not bid hard on those cards, so you pick them up for minimal gold. Conversely, avoid sending cards to the pile that help your strongest opponent. Even a 1-value card in their best color strengthens them.

walkthrough.title: The Gold Economy
walkthrough.text: Gold management separates beginners from veterans. In the Gift Phase, keep gold cards worth 2 or 3 whenever possible. Low-value gold cards worth 1 are better sent to the auction pile or given to opponents as weak gifts. Entering the Auction Phase with 6 or more gold value gives you dominant bidding power. You can win two or three critical auctions while opponents who neglected gold can only watch.

walkthrough.title: Misdirection and Feints
walkthrough.text: In the Gift Phase, the cards you give opponents are visible. Use this to mislead. If you are secretly collecting Monks, occasionally give an opponent a Monks card you do not need (a low-value 1) while keeping high-value ones hidden in your hand. Opponents may assume you are not pursuing Monks and lower their guard. In the Auction Phase, pass on a Monks card early to seem disinterested, then bid aggressively on a critical one later when opponents have spent their gold.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Removed Cards
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, cards are removed unseen. You cannot know exactly which cards were taken, but you can deduce information as the game progresses. If very few Holy Books cards appear during the Gift Phase, many were likely removed. This means the category is thin, and a small investment might win the die. Pay attention to which colors seem underrepresented — those are the categories where a few cards go a long way.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Church Card Plays
walkthrough.text: While General Tips said to play Church cards early, advanced players sometimes hold one for a calculated late strike. If you play a Church card to raise a die during the Auction Phase, opponents may not have time to respond. They have already spent their Church cards or cannot adjust the die back. A late Church card play can steal a VP that changes the winner. The risk is holding it too long and never using it. Use this tactic only when you have a specific scoring scenario in mind.

walkthrough.title: Controlling the Gold Auction Reversal
walkthrough.text: When gold cards appear in the auction, category cards become currency. Advanced players exploit this asymmetry. If you have surplus cards in a category you have already locked up — say you are winning Manuscripts by 6 points and have a spare 2-value card — use that surplus to buy gold cheaply. The key word is surplus. Never bid away cards that threaten your majority. Calculate your lead in each category before every gold auction and only spend from safe margins.

walkthrough.title: The Two-Die Victory Path
walkthrough.text: In most games, winning two high-value dice is enough to win. If you can push two dice to 5 or 6 each using Church cards and lock those categories with strong card totals, your 10-12 VP will likely beat opponents who won three dice at 2-3 each. This means you can concede entire categories early, focusing all your drafting, gold, and Church cards on just two colors. It is a concentrated, efficient path to victory — the hallmark of an experienced abbot.

summary.title: The Die Manipulation Engine
bullets: Win cheap categories and inflate their dice to 5-6 with Church cards
Lower dice in categories you concede
Two dice at 5 each (10 VP) often beats three dice at 2-3

summary.title: Auction Pile Seeding
bullets: Send cards to auction that you can buy cheaply later
Seed categories opponents are not collecting
Avoid sending cards that strengthen your rival's best color

summary.title: The Gold Economy
bullets: Prioritize keeping gold 2 and gold 3 during the Gift Phase
Send gold 1 to the auction pile or give as weak gifts
6+ gold value entering Auction Phase = dominant bidding power

summary.title: Misdirection and Feints
bullets: Give low-value cards in your secret color to mislead opponents
Pass on early auctions in your target color to seem disinterested
Bid aggressively later when opponents have spent their gold

summary.title: Reading the Removed Cards
bullets: Track which colors are underrepresented during the Gift Phase
Thin categories = small investment can win the die
Deduction improves as more cards are revealed

summary.title: Timing Your Church Card Plays
bullets: Late Church card plays catch opponents off guard
Opponents may have no Church cards left to respond
Risk: holding too long and never using it — only delay with a plan

summary.title: Controlling the Gold Auction Reversal
bullets: Bid category cards for gold only from surplus (safe margins)
Calculate your lead in each category before every gold auction
Surplus = points above what you need to maintain majority

summary.title: The Two-Die Victory Path
bullets: Winning 2 high-value dice (5-6 each) often beats 3 low-value dice
Concede weak categories early to concentrate resources
Focus all drafting, gold, and Church cards on 2 colors

entries.definition: Colored cards in five types (pigments, holy books, manuscripts, forbidden tomes, monks) with printed values of 1 to 4. Collected to compete for category dice.
Cards with values of 1, 2, or 3 used as currency during the Auction Phase to bid on category and Church cards. Worth zero victory points at end of game.
Special cards that allow you to adjust any one category die up or down by 1 (within range 1-6). Can be played as a free action on your turn in either phase.
Five colored dice, one per category, placed on the game board. Each starts at 3 and can be adjusted by Church cards. The face value equals the VP awarded to the player who wins that category.
A small board placed in the center of the table that holds the five category dice. Provides a visual reference for current die values.
A face-down pile of cards built during the Gift Phase. Each player contributes one card per turn. The pile is shuffled and auctioned off one card at a time during the Auction Phase.
The first phase of the game. The active player draws cards one at a time (players + 1) and assigns each immediately: keep 1, give 1 to each opponent, and place 1 on the auction pile.

The second phase. The auction pile is shuffled, and cards are revealed one at a time. Players bid to win each card. Category and Church cards are bid on with gold; gold cards are bid on with category cards.
Place a drawn card face down into your hand. You may keep exactly one card per turn during the Gift Phase.
Place a drawn card face up in front of an opponent. Each opponent must receive exactly one card per turn. Given cards are visible to all players.
Place a drawn card face down on the auction pile. Exactly one card must go to the pile each turn.
When a category card is revealed at auction, players bid using gold cards. Highest gold value wins. Minimum bid is 1 gold. Pass once and you are out of that auction.
When a gold card is revealed at auction, players bid using category cards from their hand. Highest total category card value wins. The spent category cards are discarded.
When a Church card is revealed at auction, players bid using gold cards, same as for category cards. The winner may play the Church card effect immediately.
Once you pass on an auction, you cannot re-enter bidding for that card. If all players pass, the auctioneer (current turn player) takes the card for free.
One of the five card categories (color-coded). Represents the inks and paints used by monastery scribes to illuminate manuscripts.
One of the five card categories (color-coded). Represents sacred religious texts collected for the monastery's library.
One of the five card categories (color-coded). Represents hand-copied texts and scrolls produced in the scriptorium.
One of the five card categories (color-coded). Represents rare and controversial texts kept hidden in the monastery's restricted collection.
One of the five card categories (color-coded). Represents the scribes and scholars working in the monastery to produce and preserve texts.
Before play begins, cards are removed unseen from the deck. 2 players: 22 removed. 3 players: 15 removed. 4 players: 8 removed. These cards stay secret all game.
The active player draws cards equal to number of players plus 1. In a 2-player game: 3 cards. 3-player: 4 cards. 4-player: 5 cards.
Having the highest total card value in a category. The player with the majority wins that category's die. Ties mean no one wins the die.
Each category die shows a value from 1 to 6, starting at 3. This value equals the VP awarded to the player who wins the majority in that category. Church cards adjust die values during play.
If players are tied in total die VP, the player who won more individual dice wins. If still tied, players share the victory.
Playing a Church card does not cost you your turn or replace any other action. It can be played at any time during your turn in either phase, in addition to your normal actions.
